title: Helicity-Resolved Spatiotemporal Mapping of Chiral Plexcitons in Helicoids
authors: Jeong Hyun Han, Sankaran Ramesh, Jaeyeon Jo, Pavel Chabera, Ryeong Myeong Kim, Sung Hoon Cho, In Han Ha, Amitav Sahu, Yoonsang Tak, Jiawei Lv, Miyoung Kim, Ki Tae Nam, Tönu Pullerits
abstract: Plasmon-exciton hybrids, or plexcitons, offer deeply subwavelength light-matter interactions with versatile pathways for energy redistribution. Incorporating chirality into such systems is particularly compelling, enabling spin-sensitive optical functionality that can operate on ultrafast timescales and within ultracompact volumes. Despite recent progress in chiral plexcitonic systems, how structural chirality and plasmon-exciton coupling determine chiroptical spectra and ultrafast energy flow remains elusive. Here we realize chiral plexcitons by functionalizing intrinsically chiral gold helicoid nanoparticles with molecular J-aggregates. Within a non-Hermitian framework, we trace the microscopic origin of the helicoid chiroptical response and its coupling to the excitonic transition, revealing how the helicity of light selectively addresses distinct hybrid responses. At the spatiotemporal extreme, we find that the gap-localized response not only enhances polarization-sensitive contrast but also strengthens the local hybrid interaction, leading to accelerated ultrafast relaxation. Together, these space-, time-, and polarization-resolved measurements provide a physically grounded and experimentally benchmarked picture of chiral plexcitonic coupling, identifying chirality as a practical control parameter for selectively steering nanoscale energy pathways and dynamics.
summary: The research investigates chiral plexcitons formed by coupling J-aggregates to gold helicoids. By employing a theoretical Non-Hermitian Hamiltonian and polarization-resolved ultrafast spectroscopy, the authors demonstrate that light's handedness selectively addresses distinct nanoscale energy states. This confirms chirality as a practical control mechanism for steering energy flow and accelerating relaxation dynamics in plasmonic nanostructures.
main_problem: The core problem is understanding how structural chirality and plasmon-exciton coupling dictate chiroptical spectra and ultrafast energy flow in plexcitonic systems.
main_result: Polarization (helicity) acts as a selective control parameter, allowing researchers to probe distinct nanoscale hybrid states—specifically accelerating relaxation pathways localized within the chiral gaps under circularly polarized light.
method: The study combines theoretical modeling using Non-Hermitian Hamiltonians with advanced experimental techniques like polarization-resolved Transient Absorption (TA) spectroscopy and Electron Energy Loss Spectroscopy (EELS).
model_or_system: The system consists of molecular J-aggregates coupled to intrinsically chiral gold helicoid nanoparticles, forming a plexcitonic structure. The physics is modeled using an extended Non-Hermitian Hamiltonian that incorporates plasmonic Electric Dipole (ED) and Magnetic Dipole (MD) modes alongside the excitonic transition.
key_observables: Chiroptical response (CD), spectral splitting into Upper/Lower Branches (UB/LB), polarization dependence of near-field intensity, and ultrafast decay kinetics ($	au$).
important_parameters: Intermediate coupling regime; helicity of light (RCP vs LCP); gap localization; sub-picosecond time scales.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the NHH framework to dissect spectra and assumes that polarization selectivity in dynamics can reveal underlying near-field spatial asymmetries, even if steady-state spectra are identical.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate spectral splitting (UB/LB), field intensity mapping showing gap localization under RCP, and time-resolved TA maps demonstrating accelerated decay kinetics specifically when probing the chiral gap region with circularly polarized light.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical system and theoretical framework (NHH) to characterizing steady-state chiroptical signatures via EELS/spectroscopy. It then moves to ultrafast dynamics, using polarization-resolved TA spectroscopy to demonstrate that helicity selectively probes distinct energy relaxation pathways within the chiral gap.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful demonstration of how light's angular momentum (helicity) can be used as an active knob to spatially and temporally resolve different coupled quantum states in nanoscale optical systems, which is highly relevant for advanced quantum photonics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
